anvaya.meaning: whose self is satisfied with knowledge and realization

translations.verbatim_quote: A person is said to be established in self-realization and is called a yogī [or mystic] when he is fully satisfied by virtue of acquired knowledge and realization. Such a person is situated in transcendence and is self-controlled. He sees everything – whether it be pebbles, stones or gold – as the same.

A person is said to be established in self-realization and is called a yogi [or mystic] when he is fully satisfied by virtue of acquired knowledge and realization. Such a person is situated in transcendence and is self-controlled. He sees everything—whether it be pebbles, stones or gold—as the same.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1389 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Book knowledge without realization of the Supreme Truth is useless. This is stated as follows:ataḥ śrī-kṛṣṇa-nāmādina bhaved grāhyam indriyaiḥsevonmukhe hi jihvādausvayam eva sphuraty adaḥ“No one can understand the transcendental nature of the name, form, quality and pastimes of Śrī Kṛṣṇa through his materially contami

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.8 A yogi, jnana-vijnana-trpta-atma, whose mind is satisfied with knowledge and realization-jnana is thorough knowledge of things presented by the scriptures, but vijnana is making those things known from the scriptures a subject of one’s own realization just as they have been presented;…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-8-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: 6.8 The yogi whose mind is satisfied with knowledge of the scriptures and personal experience, who remains immovable, who has subdued the senses, is said to be 'yukta' — established in steadfast yoga. To him, a clod of earth, a stone, and gold are alike.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.8 That Karma Yogin whose mind is content with the knowledge of the self and the knowledge of the difference, i.e., whose mind is content with the knowledge concerning the real nature of the self as well as with the knowledge of the difference of Its nature from Prakrti; ‘who is establish…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-8-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: 6.8 The Yogin who is satiated by Self-knowledge and Self-realization, who is unwavering, with the senses conquered, who looks alike on a lump of clay, a stone or gold, is said to be 'yukta' — fit for yoga.

positions.rendering: remaining immovable, unchanging — Advaita reading of unchangingness

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Translators agree on operational steadiness; the Advaita reading of kūṭa-stha as metaphysically unchanging is more emphatic than Vishishtadvaita's operational unwaveringness.

literal_meaning: The yogi whose self is satisfied with knowledge and discriminative knowledge, who is unmoving as a peak, who has fully conquered the senses, who regards a clod, a stone, and gold as equal — that one is said to be yoked (yukta).
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna gives the second equanimity-diagnostic. The yukta-yogi: satisfied with knowledge (jñāna, theoretical) and discriminative knowledge (vijñāna, experiential), unmoving as a peak (kūṭastha), senses conquered, equal toward clod-stone-gold. The threefold equality (loṣṭa-aśma-kāñcana) is the practical test of detachment from material distinctions.
